prompt: The bar chart shows the percentage of students at three language schools who used online videos, mobile vocabulary apps, printed textbooks, and speaking clubs as their main study method in 2025.
visual_facts: Main feature: online videos were the most popular method in all three schools, while speaking clubs were used by the smallest share of students.

review_units.original: The graph illustrates the learning habits of students in three schools in 2025.

comments.text: 图表类型要写具体：bar chart

review_units.rewrite: The bar chart illustrates the proportions of students at three language schools who mainly used four different study methods in 2025.

review_units.original: Overall, online videos were more popular than the other methods in all schools, and speaking clubs were not very popular.
review_units.rewrite: Overall, online videos were the most popular method in every school, whereas speaking clubs accounted for the smallest shares.

review_units.original: The percentages for textbooks in School B and School C were lower, the former was about 25% and the latter was barely 20%.
review_units.rewrite: The figures for printed textbooks in School B and School C were lower, at approximately 25% and just under 20%, respectively.

score_explanation: 主要趋势清楚，但部分表达偏泛，少数数据描述还需要更精确。
冠词和比较结构上的小错误会影响稳定 Band 7。
focus_feedback: 要稳定到 Band 7.5，先减少冠词、复数等小语法错误，并保持比较表达简洁。
要继续冲 8.0，重点筛选最重要的数据，并灵活使用 respectively、that of 等比较结构。
model_answer: The bar chart compares the proportions of students at three language schools who mainly used online videos, mobile vocabulary apps, printed textbooks, or speaking clubs in 2025.

Overall, online videos were the most popular study method in every school, whereas speaking clubs accounted for the smallest shares. Mobile vocabulary apps were also widely used, while printed textbooks were less common.

School A had the highest percentage of students using online videos, at about 55%, compared with roughly 50% in School B and 45% in School C. Vocabulary apps followed a similar pattern, although the figures were lower, ranging from around 30% to just under 40% across the three institutions.

By contrast, printed textbooks were chosen by only about a quarter of students in School B and slightly fewer in School C, while School A was marginally higher. Speaking clubs attracted the lowest proportions overall, with figures of approximately 10% to 15% across the three schools.
